title: CPR (Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation)
steps: Check the scene for safety and the person for responsiveness.
Call emergency services immediately if no response.
Place the person on their back on a firm, flat surface.
Give 30 chest compressions (2 inches deep, 100-120 bpm).
Give 2 rescue breaths if trained, or continue compressions only.
Repeat until professional help arrives or an AED is available.

title: Severe Hemorrhage
steps: Apply direct pressure to the wound with a clean cloth or gauze.
Maintain pressure without lifting the cloth to check the wound.
If the dressing becomes soaked, add move gauze on top (do not remove original).
Elevate the injury above the heart level if possible.
If bleeding is life-threatening and on a limb, apply a tourniquet 2-3 inches above the wound.
Monitor for signs of shock (pale skin, rapid pulse, shallow breathing).

title: Choking (Heimlich Maneuver)
steps: Ask 'Are you choking?' and encourage them to cough.
If they cannot breathe/cough, stand behind them and wrap your arms around their waist.
Make a fist and place it just above the navel.
Perform 5 quick, upward abdominal thrusts.
Alternate with 5 back blows between the shoulder blades.
Continue until the object is forced out or the person becomes unconscious (then start CPR).

title: Severe Burns
steps: Stop the burning process: Remove from heat or douse with water.
Run cool (not cold) water over the burn for at least 10-20 minutes.
Remove jewelry or tight clothing before swelling begins.
Cover the burn loosely with a sterile, non-stick bandage or plastic wrap.
Do NOT apply ice, butter, or ointments to a severe burn.
Do NOT pop blisters.

title: Stroke (FAST)
steps: FACE: Ask the person to smile. Does one side of the face droop?
ARMS: Ask the person to raise both arms. Does one arm drift downward?
SPEECH: Ask the person to repeat a simple phrase. Is their speech slurred or strange?
TIME: If you observe any of these signs, call emergency services immediately.
Note the time when symptoms first appeared.
Keep the person calm and lying down until help arrives.

title: Seizure Response
steps: Gently place the person on the floor and clear the area of hard or sharp objects.
Place something soft (like a folded jacket) under their head.
Turn the person onto their side to keep the airway clear.
Do NOT put anything in their mouth (risk of injury to you or them).
Do NOT restrain the person; let the seizure run its course.
Time the seizure; call emergency services if it lasts more than 5 minutes.

title: Poisoning
steps: Try to identify the substance and the amount ingested.
Call the local Poison Control Center or emergency services immediately.
Do NOT induce vomiting unless specifically instructed by a professional.
If the substance is on the skin or eyes, flush with water for 15-20 minutes.
If the person is unconscious but breathing, place them on their side.
Save the container or label to show to medical personnel.

title: Drowning / Water Rescue
steps: Get the person out of the water as quickly as possible without endangering yourself.
Check for responsiveness and breathing.
If not breathing, start rescue breathing immediately (even in the water if safe).
Perform CPR (30 compressions, 2 breaths) as soon as on a firm surface.
If person vomits, turn their head to the side to prevent choking.
Always seek medical attention, even if the person seems fine (risk of secondary drowning).

title: Anaphylaxis (Severe Allergic Reaction)
steps: Call emergency services immediately.
Check if the person is carrying an epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen).
Administer the auto-injector: Press firmly against the outer thigh and hold for 3-10 seconds.
Have the person lie flat on their back with feet elevated.
If breathing stops, begin CPR immediately.
Keep the used auto-injector to show to medical personnel.
Even if symptoms improve, the person MUST go to the hospital as a second wave of reaction is possible.

title: Hypothermia / Cold Exposure
steps: Move the person out of the cold and into a dry, warm location.
Gently remove wet clothing and replace with dry blankets or clothes.
Warm the person gradually; start with the center of the body (chest, neck, groin).
Provide warm, non-alcoholic, non-caffeinated beverages if they are conscious.
Do NOT use direct heat (hot water, heating pads) or rub limbs, as this can cause heart rhythm issues.
Keep the person lying down and monitor breathing until medical help arrives.

title: Fractures and Dislocations
steps: Do NOT attempt to move the person unless there is an immediate danger.
Do NOT try to realign the bone or push it back in.
Apply a sterile dressing to any open wounds before splinting.
Immobilize the injured area: Use a split (folded cardboard, wood) that extends beyond the joints above and below the break.
Apply ice packs to reduce swelling (do not apply directly to skin).
Check for circulation (pulse/warmth) below the injury site after splinting.

title: Diabetic Emergency
steps: If the person is conscious and can swallow, give them 15-20g of fast-acting sugar (fruit juice, soda, candy).
Wait 15 minutes and repeat if they do not feel better.
If they are unconscious: Call emergency services immediately. Do NOT put anything in their mouth.
If they are carrying a Glucagon kit, it may be administered by a trained person.
Note the time and what was given to inform the paramedics.

title: Syncope (Fainting)
steps: Lay the person flat on their back and elevate their legs about 12 inches (if no injury).
Loosen any tight clothing (collars, belts) to improve circulation.
Check for breathing and responsiveness; if they don't wake up quickly, call emergency services.
If they vomited, turn them onto their side to prevent choking.
Do NOT splash water on their face or try to give them anything to drink until fully conscious.
Keep them lying down for at least 10-15 minutes after they wake up.
